name: Maurya Empire
note: Ashoka converted to Buddhism and propagated it across Asia — a major religious shift mid-dynasty that changed the empire's entire ideological character

figures.name: Chandragupta Maurya
figures.significance: Unified India for the first time; expelled Macedonian garrisons

figures.name: Kautilya (Chanakya)
figures.significance: Author of the Arthashastra — the first systematic treatise on statecraft

figures.name: Ashoka the Great
figures.significance: Converted to Buddhism after Kalinga War; spread Buddhism to Central Asia, Sri Lanka, Southeast Asia

name: Gupta Empire
note: Hindu 'Golden Age'; Vaishnavism as court religion; classical Sanskrit literature and mathematics flourished

figures.name: Samudragupta
figures.significance: Military genius and poet; the Napoleon and Augustus of India

figures.name: Aryabhata
figures.significance: Calculated pi; proposed a heliocentric model; developed zero as a number

name: Delhi Sultanate
note: Established the Persianate Islamic cultural layer in India; Persian as court language; laid the groundwork for the Hindu-Muslim civilizational divide that defines the subcontinent to this day

figures.name: Qutb ud-Din Aibak
figures.significance: Founded the Delhi Sultanate; built the Qutb Minar

figures.name: Alauddin Khalji
figures.significance: Most powerful Delhi Sultan; repelled multiple Mongol invasions

name: Mughal Empire
note: Babur was Timurid (Turkic-Mongol); court was thoroughly Persianized. Akbar's syncretic 'Din-i-Ilahi' experiment attempted to bridge Hindu and Muslim identities — shows religion as a live political variable even within a single regime

policies.name: Din-i-Ilahi (Akbar's Divine Faith)
policies.description: Akbar's syncretic personal cult. Policy experiment — Mughal state remained Sunni Hanafi. Died with Akbar.

policies.name: Aurangzeb's Sharia Enforcement
policies.description: Reimposed jizya, destroyed Hindu temples. Policy within Sunni Hanafi ideology — not an ideological shift, but accelerated Mughal collapse.

figures.name: Babur
figures.significance: Timurid prince who conquered India; his Baburnama is one of the great autobiographies

figures.name: Akbar the Great
figures.significance: Consolidated the empire; policy of religious tolerance; abolished jizya

figures.name: Shah Jahan
figures.significance: Built the Taj Mahal; apex of Mughal Persian-Indian cultural synthesis

figures.name: Aurangzeb
figures.significance: Largest territorial extent; Sharia policies alienated Hindus; his death began the collapse

name: Satavahana Empire
note: First great Deccan empire; established the bridge between northern Aryan and southern Dravidian cultures. Patronised both Hinduism and Buddhism. Their trade networks stretched to Rome.

figures.name: Simuka
figures.significance: Founded the Satavahana dynasty after defeating the Maurya successor states

figures.name: Gautamiputra Satakarni
figures.significance: Greatest Satavahana ruler; defeated the Western Satraps (Saka rulers); the first major Indian king to claim exclusively Indian descent against foreign rulers

name: Harsha Empire
note: Last major Hindu-Buddhist empire of the Ganges plain before the Islamic conquests. Harsha personally wrote Sanskrit plays and patronised the scholar Xuanzang (the Chinese pilgrim whose journey inspired Journey to the West). His empire collapsed immediately after his death, leaving no successors.

figures.name: Harshavardhana
figures.significance: Unified northern India after the Gupta collapse; patron of Nalanda university; personally practiced Buddhism while ruling a Hindu state; wrote three Sanskrit plays

name: Pala Empire
note: The last great Buddhist empire of India; patrons of Nalanda and Vikramashila universities — the apex of Indian Buddhist scholarship. Their fall to the Ghurids (1203) and the subsequent destruction of Nalanda (1203) effectively ended institutional Buddhism in India.

figures.name: Dharmapala
figures.significance: Second and greatest Pala king; expanded the empire from Bengal to Afghanistan; built Vikramashila university

figures.name: Devapala
figures.significance: Extended Pala power to its greatest extent; maintained relations with Srivijaya in Southeast Asia; endowed Nalanda monastery

name: Vijayanagara Empire
note: The last great Hindu empire; for three centuries it was the bulwark of Hindu civilisation against the Muslim Deccan sultanates. Founded by brothers Harihara and Bukka after escaping Delhi Sultanate captivity. The Battle of Talikota (1565) destroyed the capital Hampi — the ruins remain one of India's great historical sites.

figures.name: Krishnadevaraya
figures.significance: Greatest Vijayanagara king; defeated all the Deccan sultanates; patronised Telugu literature — the golden age of Telugu culture; Hampi at its peak rivalled Rome in size

figures.name: Rama Raya
figures.significance: Manipulated the Deccan sultanates against each other until they united and destroyed him at Talikota; his death ended Vijayanagara as a great power

name: Bahmani Sultanate
note: The first major Muslim sultanate of the Deccan; founded by a revolt against Delhi. Fragmented into five successor sultanates (Adil Shahi, Nizam Shahi, Qutb Shahi, Imad Shahi, Barid Shahi) which fought Vijayanagara and each other for two centuries. Bidar was its capital.

figures.name: Hasan Gangu Bahman
figures.significance: A soldier of Afghan origin who revolted against Delhi and founded the Bahmani Sultanate; the first independent Muslim power in the Deccan

figures.name: Mahmud Gawan
figures.significance: Persian scholar-statesman who modernised the Bahmani state; established a college at Bidar; executed on false treason charges — his death accelerated the sultanate's collapse

name: Maratha Empire
note: The Marathas challenged Mughal dominance and nearly unified India under Hindu rule before the British arrived. At their peak (1760) they controlled most of the subcontinent. The Third Battle of Panipat (1761) against the Afghans shattered Maratha power and opened the door for British expansion.

figures.name: Shivaji Bhonsle
figures.significance: Founded the Maratha kingdom through guerrilla warfare against Mughal and Bijapur forces; crowned Chhatrapati (sovereign) 1674; created the concept of Hindavi Swarajya (Hindu self-rule)

figures.name: Bajirao I
figures.significance: Greatest Peshwa (prime minister); never lost a battle; expanded Maratha territory from the Deccan to Delhi; the dominant military mind of 18th-century India

name: Sikh Empire
note: Ranjit Singh unified the Sikh misls (confederacies) into the last major pre-British Indian empire. The Sikh army (Khalsa) trained by European officers was the most modern in Asia. The two Anglo-Sikh Wars ended Sikh independence — the Koh-i-Noor diamond was ceded to the British Crown.

figures.name: Ranjit Singh
figures.significance: The Lion of the Punjab; unified the Sikh misls at age 19; built a secular empire that welcomed Hindus, Muslims, and Europeans; kept the British at bay through diplomacy for 40 years

figures.name: Hari Singh Nalwa
figures.significance: Greatest Sikh general; conquered Peshawar and Kashmir; extended Sikh rule to the Khyber Pass — the first time India's northwest frontier was secured from Central Asian invasions
